Bioelectrical impedance analysis
Bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) is a commonly used method to estimate
someone's body composition by running an electrical current through the body via
electrodes that pass from foot to foot, hand to foot or hand to hand. The most common
examples of BIA are the digital body fat scales you can stand on, sometimes with hand
grips, like those from Tanita. By sending an electrical current through the body via the
feet, the hands, or both, the device measures the opposition to the current -
impedance. The lowest impedance value can be used to predict fat-free mass, because
your organs, muscles and blood all have a high amount of water and electrolytes that
easily conduct the current. Adipose tissue, your fat mass, has a poorer electrical
conductivity. So with some math, BIA allows us to estimate someone's body fat
percentage. BIA is very safe and rapidly becoming more popular because of its ease of
use and non-invasiveness.

A BIA scanner (InBody model).

While older single-frequency BIA scans could only distinguish between lean and non-
lean tissue with impractically poor accuracy, newer multi-frequency BIA methods can
also estimate body water and bone and thereby divide the body into 3 or 4
compartments rather than 2, greatly increasing their accuracy. When multiple
frequencies are used, the higher frequencies can estimate both intracellular and
extracellular water, because the higher-frequency current can penetrate cell


--- Page 110 ---
membranes. Low frequencies cannot pass cell membranes, so they primarily measure
the presence of extracellular water.

Of the commercially available body composition techniques, multi-frequency BIA
measurements can come close to gold-standard 4C techniques to measure body
composition in bodybuilders and can be even be slightly more reliable than DXA scans,
though other research finds multi-frequency BIA scans, including the InBody,
significantly deviate from DXA scans in lean, athletic populations [2, 3]. Single-
frequency BIA does not correspond well with DXA scans at all. Most 3C and 4C body fat
percentage estimates are accurate within 2%, but 5% error in either direction occurs in
some scans.

Unfortunately, many 3C or 4C methods for BIA and BIS scans require an adjunct
measure of body volume or body water, which requires methods typically restricted to
laboratories. Most commercially available body fat scales are single-frequency, so they
have poor accuracy. They're also generally not calibrated for muscular individuals. BIA
scales without hand grips are particularly unreliable generally [2]. You need a device
that sends the electrical current through both your hands and feet for reasonable
accuracy. The best commonly available multi-frequency BIA scanner is currently
arguably the InBody. Although it's only a 2C model, the InBody has been reported to
generally estimate body fat percentage within 2% accuracy of 4C models in untrained
individuals. In trained populations, research finds significant discrepancies between
InBody and DXA scan results [2, 3], though some research finds reasonable agreement
with 4C models. Even for multi-frequency BIA scanners with a decent total body body
fat percentage estimate, the appendicular estimates are too unreliable for practical use
[2], and weekly or even monthly changes are generally too variable or insensitive to be
used to measure progress over time.

Even the best scales suffer from one major, inevitable problem inherent to BIA: the
measurement is strongly affected by hydration status. If you're dehydrated or bloated,


--- Page 111 ---
the scale will tell you your body fat percentage has changed when of course it has not.
So you'll get different readings before and after going to the gym as a result of the loss
of electrolytes and sweat and you'll get different readings before and after having a
drink. To account for this, it is best to perform the measurements fasted and after
having gone to the bathroom.

In practice, BIA is not very useful, unless you happen to have access to 3C or 4C
methods multiple times per week, fasted in the morning. Single-frequency BIA scans
are practically useless. Commercial multi-frequency BIA scanners like the InBody can
be used to roughly estimate someone's body fat percentage to calculate their starting
macros for a program, but BIA measurements are generally too unreliable to monitor
weekly changes in body composition. Skinfold calipers and circumference
measurements are better for week-to-week progress measurements.

A method very similar to BIA is bioimpedance spectroscopy (BIS). They only differ in
how they mathematically calculate body fat percentage based on the impedance
measurements.

Electrical impedance myography (EIM), a method most commonly used by the Skulpt to
estimate body fat percentage, is also similar to multi-frequency BIA in terms of accuracy
at measuring body composition. It correlates only moderately well with DXA scans even
in untrained individuals and there's no research on trained individuals.

In the next sections, we'll go into less readily available measurement methods of body
fat percentage.

Absorptiometry (incl. DXA)
Absorptiometry is an imaging technique that involves scanning the entire body or a
portion of the body with a photon beam. Dual X-ray photon absorptiometry (DXA,


--- Page 112 ---
formerly DEXA) is the most common type. With the knowledge that different tissues
have different densities, DXA scans can be used to estimate 3 compartments of the
body's mass: bone mass, non-bone lean body mass and fat mass.

Radiation exposure to subjects is very low, the procedure is relatively quick and the
accuracy of the body fat percentage estimate is high. Of the fancier body fat percentage
measurement techniques, DXA is generally the most readily available and affordable
with scans costing somewhere around $50.

However, DXA can be influenced by food intake, electrolytes, hydration status, creatine
[2, 3] and daily activities, because the scanner cannot differentiate between muscle and
fluid mass. For example, drinking a large glass of water right before the test will make a
DXA scan think you gained that weight in lean body mass, which is technically correct of
course, but it's not what we want to measure. So for maximum measurement reliability,
you should standardize your measurement conditions [2]. It's generally most convenient
to test yourself fasted in the morning.

While DXA scan estimates are typically within a few or even one percent of your actual
body fat percentage and test-retest accuracy is extremely high (ICC > 0.99) [2], they
can be wildly off in certain cases. For example, the DXA estimate of Bozhidar I


--- Page 113 ---
Boradzhiev's body fat percentage in the condition illustrated below during his contest
prep with Dutch PT Course host Richard Bell was 17.8%. Unless he had a bulge of fat
where his kidneys are supposed to be, that's not very likely. Again though, these are
exceptions to what is normally a reasonable estimate of body fat.


--- Page 114 ---
To measure week-to-week progress, DXA scans are not accurate enough, in particular
not the segmental analyses such as arm fat-free mass. DXA scan estimates of changes
in body composition generally differ by 1-3% and sometimes up to 5% from 4C-model
estimates in bulking strength trainees. That's not bad for group averages, but for an
individual measuring short-term progress, the error margin is often as large as the
actual change in body composition values. DXA scans are thus considerably worse than
properly used skinfold calipers or tape measures to track someone's progress. This
actually goes for all commercially available body composition testing methods: none are
accurate enough to reliably track changes of only a few percent in strength trainees'
body composition.

Thus, is it worth getting a DXA scan? Probably not. In practice, DXA scans may be
useful get a starting estimate of someone's body fat percentage or as a very long-term
measure of body composition progress, similar to multi-frequency BIA scans. However,
the practical utility of the scan information is generally slim compared to a good visual
estimate.


--- Page 115 ---
Densitometry
This is the measurements of total body density through determination of body volume
by underwater weighing, helium displacement or a combination of water displacement
by the body and air displacement by the head. These measurements can be used to
determine body density, which in turn allows physicists to calculate the percentage of
body fat and fat-free mass. There are 2 densitometry techniques.
1.
Underwater weighing: Measurements are precise and were long considered
the gold standard for determining body composition but must be conducted in
the laboratory and this method is not appropriate for children or the elderly,
because, well, see the image below.


--- Page 116 ---
2.
Air-displacement plethysmography (ADP): Values of body density obtained by
ADP correlate well with underwater weighing for most populations.

In short, should you have access to it, densitometry is in theory a great way to measure
your body composition.

Computerized (axial) tomography (CT/CAT scans)
During CAT scans, computerized processing of X-ray data generates an image. Fat,
lean tissue and bone can be identified by their characteristic density-frequency
distribution. This method determines regional body composition, including the ratio of
intra-abdominal to subcutaneous fat and the size of the liver, spleen and kidney. The
body composition estimates are precise when done carefully in a good laboratory, but
the equipment is costly and requires extensive technological know-how, so this method
is generally not a cost-effective way to track body composition progress.


--- Page 117 ---
CAT scanning: great, should you have affordable access to it.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
MRI scanning is performed in a machine that looks much like a CAT scanner. However,
MRI is based upon the principle that when an external magnetic field is applied across
the body, atomic nuclei (cell cores) behave like magnets - that is, the nuclei attempt to
align with the field. The nuclei also absorb radio frequency waves directed into the body
and in turn change their orientation in the magnetic field. Once the radio wave is turned
off, the activated nuclei will emit the signal absorbed; this emitted signal is used to
develop images by a computer that can determine the type of tissue the signal comes
from based on the signal's characteristics. MRI equipment is very expensive, but if you
have access to it, it is a great way to determine both body composition and regional
muscle mass, making it popular in exercise science research.

Ultrasound/ultrasonography
This approach uses an instrument in which electrical energy is converted in a probe to
high-frequency ultrasonic energy. Subsequent transmission of these sound waves
through various tissues can be used to calculate tissue thickness. You'll probably know


--- Page 118 ---
this measurement technique from its use during pregnancy. Unfortunately, it's not that
useful to calculate someone's body fat percentage in practice, because its manual
measurements require high measurement proficiency, and as you learned, even then
you're not much better off than with skinfold calipers. Unless you happen to have
convenient access to ultrasound and a skilled technician, you're better off with the more
portable, cheaper and user-friendlier skinfold calipers.

Ultrasound scanning

Infrared interactance
Infrared interactance is based upon the principle that when material is exposed to
infrared light, the light is absorbed, reflected or transmitted depending upon the
scattering and absorption properties of the material. To assess body composition, a
probe that acts as an infrared transmitter and detector is placed on the skin. Infrared
light of two wavelengths is transmitted by the probe to a depth of 4 cm. This method
suffers from the same problem as ultrasound measurement, so it provides little
advantage over skinfold calipers.


--- Page 119 ---
Total body water (TBW)
TBW is used as index of human body composition based on findings that water is not
present in stored triglycerides but occupies an average of 73.2% of fat-free mass. To
perform TBW measurement, a specified isotope is ingested or injected; then, following
an equilibration period, a sampling is made of the concentration of the tracer in a
selected biological fluid. TBW is calculated and the extracellular fluid can be estimated:
the difference between the two equals fat-free mass. This is a difficult, invasive and
costly procedure with limited precision, so it's not advisable to use it as a measure of
body composition progress.

Total body potassium
40K, a naturally occurring isotope, is found in a known amount (0.012%) in intra-cellular
water and is not present in stored triacylglycerols. These facts allow fat-free mass to be
estimated by the external counting of gamma rays emitted by 40K. While theoretically
very accurate, the instrument for counting 40K is prohibitively expensive and difficult to
operate to be used as a practical method of body composition measurement.

Urinary creatinine excretion
Creatinine is, as you've learned in the course topic on protein, the product resulting from
the non-enzymatic hydrolysis of free creatine, which is liberated during the
dephosphorylation of creatine phosphate. Most creatine phosphate is located in skeletal
muscle; therefore, urinary creatinine excretion can be related to muscle mass. However,
it requires 24 hours of urine collection and even then, the estimation of muscle mass is
not very accurate due to large individual variability in creatinine excretion and dietary
effects, so it's not very useful in practice.


--- Page 120 ---
3-methylhistidine excretion
Urinary 3-methylhistidine has been suggested as a useful predictor of human body
composition because this amino acid is located principally in the muscle and cannot be
reused after its release from catabolized myofibrillar proteins. However, this
measurement requires a long period of urine control with limited precision, because 3-
methylhistidine's excretion is influenced by non-skeletal muscle protein turnover in the
skin and gastrointestinal tract and meat consumption. So it's not very useful in practice.

Neutron activation analysis
This is the only technique currently available for measurement of multi-elemental
composition of the human body. Low radiation doses produce isotopic atoms in tissues;
the induced nuclides permit measurement of many elements, such as nitrogen, calcium
and sodium. While extremely precise as a measurement of body composition, you will
only rarely see it in use due to its high technological and financial requirements.

Body composition tracking for women
It is often said that the body composition of women and in particular their water retention
fluctuates dramatically over the menstrual cycle to the extent that body composition
cannot be compared week to week and rather should be compared month to month in
each phase of the menstrual cycle. Well controlled research shows that body
composition, including hydration status, is actually quite constant across the menstrual
cycle [2]. Skinfold calipers, DXA scans and ultrasound body fat estimates are consistent
across each phase of the menstrual cycle in physically active women. So body
composition changes can generally be measured in the same way in men and women:
measures like bodyweight and skinfold thicknesses can still be accurately tracked on a
week to week basis. BIA readings seem to be more affected but you should normally
still see the weekly average BF% go down during a cut.


--- Page 121 ---
Body composition tracking conclusions
It's generally not worth getting expensive body composition testing done. Of the
commercially available methods, DXA and InBody scans may give you a decent
estimate of your body fat percentage, but the estimate may not be better than an
experienced visual estimate. No body composition testing method is accurate enough to
reliably detect the small changes in body composition that typically happen in strength
trainees over the course of a week; when bulking, nothing is very accurate even over a
6-week program, except maybe in novices or trainees on drugs. Instead of trying to
track your exact body fat percentage, you're generally much better off properly using
high-quality skinfold calipers or even just a tape measure combined with daily,
standardized weigh-ins to measure your week-to-week progression. Be flexible with
how you conduct the measurements. There is no one-size-fits-all method. Rather,
consistency is everything. You may have to track medians or modes of your skinfold
measurements or different locations.


--- Page 122 ---
Learning objectives
For the exam you should be able to do the following.
• Explain what the different components of energy expenditure are and which
things can affect them.
• Judge the accuracy of different measurement methods of body fat percentage.
• Recommend whether individuals should cut, bulk or maintain based on their
specifics.
• Recommend daily/weekly average energy intakes to individuals based on their
specifics.
• Adjust an individual's energy intake based on their body composition data.
• Explain what energy, calories, thermodynamics and the energy-balance principle
are.
• Explain the difference between adipose tissue loss and fat oxidation.
• Calculate total energy intake from food labels or meal plans.
• Retrospectively calculate a person's energy balance based on 2 body
composition measurements over time.
• Explain why it takes more than 3500 kcal to lose a pound of body fat.
• Explain why being weight stable does not necessarily mean you're in energy
balance.
• Explain the difference between metabolic damage and adaptive thermogenesis.
• Explain why people yoyo-diet and why people incorrectly seem to have an
internal bodyfat setpoint.
• Explain how refeeds affect energy balance.
